title: Low Cardiac Output Syndrome after Cardiac Surgery

content.text: Low cardiac output syndrome is inadequate cardiac output for tissue oxygen demand after cardiac surgery. It may result from myocardial stunning, ischaemia/graft failure, poor ventricular function, tamponade, hypovolaemia, RV failure, valve dysfunction, arrhythmia, pacing dyssynchrony, vasoplegia with maldistributed flow, or excessive afterload. The bedside picture is falling perfusion: cool skin, low urine output, rising lactate, low SvO2/ScvO2, altered mentation when awake, narrow pulse pressure and escalating vasoactive support.

content.definition: A state after cardiac surgery where cardiac output is insufficient to maintain organ perfusion, commonly reflected by low cardiac index, rising lactate, oliguria and escalating support.
Cardiac output indexed to body surface area; normal adult range is roughly 2.5–4.0 L/min/m2, with concern often <2.2 L/min/m2 in shock context.

rows: Narrow pulse pressure and cool peripheries
Low stroke volume and vasoconstriction.
Assess volume, rhythm, pacing, echo need, inotrope plan.
Rising lactate
Oxygen delivery inadequate for demand.
Escalate, check Hb, oxygenation, CO/CI, bleeding, tamponade.
Low urine output
Renal hypoperfusion or AKI.
Check MAP/flow, CVP, fluid balance, nephrotoxins, catheter patency.
Low SvO2/ScvO2
High extraction due to low oxygen delivery.
Assess CO, Hb, SaO2, metabolic demand and shock cause.

content.text: Escalate urgently for suspected LCOS with MAP <60–65 mmHg, CI <2.0–2.2 L/min/m2 if measured, lactate rising, UO <0.5 mL/kg/hr, new arrhythmia, suspected tamponade, or escalating inotrope/vasopressor requirement.

Cardiopulmonary bypass, cross-clamp ischaemia, cardioplegic arrest, reperfusion injury and pre-existing ventricular disease can produce myocardial stunning. The ventricle may be temporarily weak even when grafts and valves are technically satisfactory. Contractility often worsens over the first several hours after surgery before improving, but this expected pattern must be separated from correctable catastrophe such as tamponade, bleeding, graft failure or valve dysfunction.

LCOS is not one disease. LV failure produces pulmonary congestion, low MAP, low cardiac index and high filling pressures. RV failure produces high CVP, low LV filling, hypotension, hepatic/renal congestion and sensitivity to hypoxia, hypercarbia and high PVR. Hypovolaemia produces low filling and low stroke volume. Tamponade produces high filling pressures but impaired filling. Arrhythmias reduce rate efficiency or AV synchrony. Excessive afterload makes a weak ventricle eject poorly.

Treatment depends on phenotype. A hypovolaemic patient may need volume/blood. A vasoplegic patient may need vasopressor. LV systolic failure may need inotrope and afterload optimisation. RV failure may need preload optimisation, pulmonary vasodilation, avoidance of hypoxia/hypercarbia/acidosis, inotrope and sometimes mechanical support. Tamponade needs decompression. No single infusion fixes every low output state.

items: Confirm the low-flow picture using MAP, pulse pressure, urine output, lactate, skin perfusion, mental state if awake, SvO2/ScvO2 and vasoactive trend.
Check rhythm and pacing. Correct bradycardia, tachyarrhythmia, loss of capture, AV dyssynchrony or heart block.
Check volume and bleeding: drains, Hb, coagulation, fluid balance, CVP/PAOP trend and echo.
Differentiate LV failure, RV failure, vasoplegia, hypovolaemia and tamponade using waveform patterns and echo where available.
Escalate early for inotrope/vasopressor optimisation, echo, surgical review, cath lab, IABP/Impella/ECMO consideration depending on cause and severity.

content.text: Low MAP is not always low cardiac output. Vasoplegia can produce low MAP with warm peripheries and high/normal output; LCOS usually shows poor flow markers.
Narrow pulse pressure is often more useful than systolic BP alone when recognising low stroke volume.
Fix rhythm before escalating inotrope blindly. A patient in complete heart block or AF with RVR may improve dramatically when rate and AV synchrony are corrected.
Do not give repeated fluid boluses to RV failure or tamponade without reassessment. High CVP and falling output can worsen with excessive volume.
The dangerous trend is rising support with worsening perfusion: more noradrenaline, less urine, higher lactate, cooler skin and narrower arterial waveform.

rows: Arterial line
MAP, pulse pressure, waveform contour.
Narrow waveform suggests low stroke volume; MAP alone may hide low flow.
CVP
Right-sided filling pressure trend.
Low CVP may suggest hypovolaemia; high CVP may suggest RV failure/tamponade/PEEP effects.
PA catheter
CI, PA pressures, PAOP, SvO2, SVR/PVR.
Helps phenotype shock when available.
Echo
Ventricular function, filling, tamponade, valve function.
Essential when numbers conflict or deterioration is unexplained.
Blood gas
Lactate, pH, Hb, electrolytes, oxygenation.
Tracks oxygen debt and reversible causes.

content.text: IABP may support coronary perfusion and reduce LV afterload in selected LV failure/ischaemia phenotypes. VA ECMO may be required for refractory cardiogenic shock or failure to separate from bypass. Device decisions are senior-led but nurses must recognise the trajectory early.

rows: Cardiac index
Normal 2.5–4.0 L/min/m2; concern often <2.2 L/min/m2 with hypoperfusion.
Low CI plus high lactate/oliguria supports LCOS.
SvO2
Typical 60–80%; concern <60% in shock context.
Low value suggests increased extraction from inadequate delivery.
ScvO2
Often ~65–75%; trend useful.
Lower than baseline with rising lactate suggests inadequate oxygen delivery.
Lactate
Should trend down; >2 and rising is concern; ≥4 high concern.
Persistent rise requires active shock evaluation.
Urine output
≥0.5 mL/kg/hr.
Low output supports poor renal perfusion if catheter patent.
Pulse pressure
Often >25–30 mmHg in acceptable stroke volume; narrowing is concerning.
Trend matters more than single value.
MAP
Usually 65–80 mmHg after routine CABG/valve surgery; often 70–90 mmHg after complex aortic surgery or chronic hypertension if ordered.
Treat the number in context: lactate, urine output, mentation/sedation, skin perfusion, SvO2/ScvO2, echo and surgical goals.
Urine output
≥0.5 mL/kg/hr; commonly ≥30 mL/hr in average adult; rising concern if <0.3 mL/kg/hr for 2 hours.
Low UO with hypotension and narrow pulse pressure suggests low flow until proven otherwise.
Lactate
Usually expected to trend down; concern if >2 mmol/L and rising; high concern if ≥4 mmol/L or rising despite support.
After CPB, lactate may be elevated, but persistent rise suggests oxygen debt, shock, bleeding, tamponade, severe vasoplegia or poor cardiac output.
Potassium
Common rhythm target 4.0–4.5 mmol/L in CTICU unless renal failure/hyperkalaemia risk.
Low K increases ectopy, AF, VT risk and digoxin sensitivity.
Magnesium
Common rhythm target ≥0.8 mmol/L; many CTICUs target 1.0 mmol/L in AF/VT risk.
Low Mg promotes ventricular ectopy and AF; replacement is common post bypass.

quiz.question: Which combination best suggests low cardiac output syndrome?
options: Cool peripheries, oliguria, rising lactate and narrow pulse pressure
Warm well-perfused patient with falling lactate
quiz.explanation: LCOS is a low-flow state causing tissue hypoperfusion.

quiz.question: Why is vasoplegia not the same as LCOS?
options: Vasoplegia may have low SVR with preserved/high output, while LCOS is inadequate flow
quiz.explanation: Both can cause hypotension but the haemodynamic phenotype and treatment differ.

quiz.question: In suspected RV failure, which factor can worsen pulmonary vascular resistance and RV output?
quiz.explanation: Hypoxia, hypercarbia and acidosis increase PVR and can worsen RV failure.
